articles.title: Petition calls for defibrillators to be mandatory in all gyms
articles.excerpt: A parliamentary petition is calling for every gym and exercise establishment to be legally required to have a defibrillator available.
articles.summary: The petition, created by Eleanor Felton-Page, asks the Government to make automated external defibrillators mandatory in all gyms and exercise venues. It is due to close on 13 October 2026.
articles.content_html:
A parliamentary petition is calling for defibrillators to become a legal requirement in every gym and exercise establishment.

The petition was created by Eleanor Felton-Page and is open for signatures until 13 October 2026.

It asks the Government to require gyms and other exercise venues to make an automated external defibrillator, commonly known as an AED, readily available in case somebody suffers a cardiac arrest.

Call for quicker access to lifesaving equipment

The petition argues that rapid access to a defibrillator could improve a person's chances of survival when cardiac arrest happens during or shortly after exercise.

Its supporting statement claims that more than 90% of sudden cardiac arrests involving young athletes occur during or immediately after exercise.

It also states that using an AED within three to five minutes could increase survival rates significantly. These figures are claims presented by the petition organiser and have not been independently verified by Rochdale Daily.

What is a defibrillator?

An automated external defibrillator is a portable device designed to analyse a person's heart rhythm and, where appropriate, deliver an electric shock to help restore a normal rhythm.

The devices provide spoken or visual instructions and can be used by members of the public while emergency help is on its way.

Anyone who believes a person is in cardiac arrest should call 999 immediately, begin CPR when instructed and send somebody to locate the nearest defibrillator where possible.

How to sign the petition

People wishing to support the proposal can sign the petition on the UK Parliament website.

Under the parliamentary petitions system, petitions that reach 10,000 signatures normally receive a Government response, while those reaching 100,000 signatures are considered for debate in Parliament.

This report is based on the wording of UK Parliament petition 764204. Medical and statistical claims attributed to the petition reflect the organiser's published case for the proposal.

articles.title: Sian Astley pledges 'day one' request to open grooming-gang files
articles.excerpt: Reform UK mayoral candidate Sian Astley has pledged to make a first-day request for Greater Manchester records connected with historic grooming-gang cases to be opened to greater scrutiny.
articles.summary: Sian Astley says she would seek greater transparency over Greater Manchester grooming-gang records if elected mayor, although the release of police and safeguarding material would remain subject to legal and operational restrictions.
articles.content_html:
Reform UK’s candidate for Mayor of Greater Manchester has pledged to make a “day one” request for records connected with historic grooming-gang cases to be opened to greater public scrutiny.

Sian Astley made the commitment while sharing a report by The Telegraph on her mayoral campaign Facebook page on 2 July.

“I do pledge this. Day 1 request.”

Astley described the issue as a long-running political problem in Greater Manchester and accused Labour of failing to deal with it adequately.

She said that, if elected, she would seek to ensure the matter was addressed and the relevant records examined.

The supplied campaign statement does not specify precisely which police, council, safeguarding or other institutional records would be covered by the request, how they would be reviewed or which organisation would determine what could lawfully be published.

What powers would the mayor have?

The Mayor of Greater Manchester also carries out Police and Crime Commissioner responsibilities, including setting policing priorities and holding the Chief Constable of Greater Manchester Police to account.

However, the Chief Constable remains operationally independent and is responsible for day-to-day policing decisions.

A mayor could request reviews, seek explanations and press relevant organisations for greater transparency, but could not automatically publish every police, council or safeguarding record personally.

Any disclosure would need to comply with laws and restrictions protecting victims, witnesses, personal information, ongoing investigations and court proceedings.

Importance to Rochdale voters

The pledge is directly relevant to Rochdale because the borough forms part of the Greater Manchester mayoral area and would be covered by the policing and accountability responsibilities of the next mayor.

Residents across Rochdale borough will be able to vote in the Greater Manchester mayoral election on Thursday 30 July 2026.

Astley is standing as the official Reform UK candidate.

The proposal remains a campaign pledge and has not been adopted as Greater Manchester policy.

This report is based on a public Facebook statement shared by Sian Astley’s mayoral campaign, a Telegraph report displayed within that post and official information published by the Greater Manchester Combined Authority. A direct link to the Facebook post or Telegraph article was not visible in the supplied screenshot.
articles.source_name: Greater Manchester Combined Authority candidate announcement
source_names: Greater Manchester Combined Authority candidate announcement
Greater Manchester Combined Authority mayoral powers guidance
articles.legal_disclaimer: This article reports a political campaign pledge concerning historic child-sexual-exploitation records. The pledge is not current Greater Manchester policy. Any review or disclosure would remain subject to victim safeguarding, data-protection law, court restrictions, operational policing independence and the rights of people who have not been charged or convicted.

articles.title: Temporary fire station in Heywood approved during redevelopment
articles.excerpt: Greater Manchester Fire and Rescue Service has secured approval for a temporary fire station in Heywood, ensuring continuous emergency services while the existing facility undergoes significant redevelopment.
articles.content_html:
ONGOING STORY — This report brings together confirmed developments from multiple sources.

Greater Manchester Fire and Rescue Service (GMFRS) has received approval to establish a temporary fire station in Heywood, facilitating the redevelopment of the existing facility. The new structure will be situated behind the current station on Middleton Road, ensuring that emergency response services remain operational throughout the rebuilding process.

The existing fire station, which has served the Heywood community for nearly 60 years, is scheduled for demolition as part of this redevelopment initiative. The temporary station will consist of a two-storey modular building, equipped with a bay for one fire engine, while an additional outbuilding will house any specialist vehicles. This arrangement is intended to guarantee that emergency services continue without interruption during the construction period.

Planning documents reveal that the temporary fire station is strategically located just 400 metres from Junction 19 of the M62 motorway, positioning it well for rapid emergency response. GMFRS has stated that the new facility will be a modern, fit-for-purpose community fire station, designed to meet both current and future operational needs. It will also enhance facilities for staff training and community engagement, reflecting the service's commitment to adapting to the evolving demands of the community.

This redevelopment is part of a wider £38 million Estates Strategy by GMFRS, aimed at upgrading and modernising several fire stations across the Greater Manchester area. The strategy underscores the importance of ensuring that fire services can provide optimal support to residents, adapting to changing community needs and operational requirements. A separate planning application for the demolition of the existing station and the construction of the new community fire station is expected to be submitted shortly.

The anticipated new facility will include a training tower, vehicle parking, and a dedicated training yard, which will further enhance GMFRS's capabilities in the region. For local families, businesses, and schools, the uninterrupted provision of emergency services during the redevelopment is crucial, as it ensures that safety and response times remain unaffected. Local residents and stakeholders are encouraged to monitor the progress of these plans, as the redevelopment will play a vital role in maintaining safety and emergency response in Heywood. Updates will be disseminated through GMFRS and local news outlets as the project advances.

articles.title: Service Changes on Rochdale Line Due to Planned Works
articles.excerpt: Planned improvement works on the Rochdale line will affect services until late August, with additional lift outages at Central Park and Queens Road tram stops.
articles.content_html:
Planned works on the Rochdale line will result in service changes from Monday, 18 May, until Wednesday, 19 August. Essential improvement works are set to take place at the Prestwich Metrolink stop. Although tram services will not be directly affected, passengers may notice an increased presence of staff at this location during the improvement period.

In addition to the works at Prestwich, there are ongoing lift outages at two tram stops. As of Friday, 10 July, the lift at Central Park, which serves the platform towards Rochdale via Oldham, is out of service. Customers travelling towards Oldham can access the Manchester-bound platform via lift from The Gateway and then use the track crossing to reach the Oldham-bound platform.

Similarly, the lift at Queens Road tram stop is also out of service as of Friday, 10 July. Passengers requiring step-free access are advised to contact the Metrolink contact centre for assistance with journey planning. The contact centre can be reached at 0161 244 1000, with lines open from 7am to 8pm on weekdays and 8am to 8pm on weekends and Bank Holidays.

Passengers are encouraged to plan their journeys accordingly, as these changes may affect travel times and accessibility. It is advisable to check the latest updates on the Transport for Greater Manchester (TfGM) travel alerts page for any further developments regarding service changes and lift availability.

The planned works at Prestwich are part of ongoing improvements to enhance the Metrolink network, ensuring better service reliability for passengers. TfGM continues to monitor the situation and will provide updates as necessary.

For more information on travel disruptions and updates, visit the TfGM travel alerts page at TfGM Travel Alerts.

articles.title: Oulder Hill pupils discuss democracy during visit to Rochdale Town Hall
articles.excerpt: Pupils from Oulder Hill Leadership Academy met the Mayor and Mayoress of Rochdale during an educational visit to the Mayor’s Parlour and council chamber.
articles.summary: Mayor Councillor Faisal Rana and Mayoress Iram Faisal welcomed Oulder Hill pupils to Rochdale Town Hall, where students asked questions about civic leadership, local democracy and how the council serves the community.
articles.content_html:
Pupils from Oulder Hill Leadership Academy have visited Rochdale Town Hall to learn more about civic leadership, local democracy and the work of the borough council.

The students were welcomed to the Mayor’s Parlour and council chamber by the Mayor of Rochdale, Councillor Faisal Rana, and Mayoress Iram Faisal.

During the visit, pupils were given an opportunity to see some of the town hall’s civic rooms and sit inside the chamber where councillors debate and make decisions affecting communities across the borough.

The students asked questions about the role of the mayor, how local democracy works and how Rochdale Borough Council serves local residents.

They also spoke about their own ambitions and aspirations for the future.

Councillor Rana described it as an “absolute pleasure” to welcome the pupils and praised their confidence, curiosity and enthusiasm.

A personal connection to Oulder Hill

The mayor said the visit held particular significance for him because his own children attended Oulder Hill, with one having served as the school’s head boy.

He said seeing a new generation of pupils sitting in the chamber where important decisions are made for the borough was a special moment.

Councillor Rana congratulated the pupils for representing their school and thanked staff for helping to make the visit a success.

He said the young people should be proud of themselves and expressed his hope that they would go on to achieve their ambitions in the years ahead.

Learning about local government

Educational visits to civic buildings can give pupils a closer look at how local decisions are made and how elected representatives, council officers and ceremonial office holders serve their communities.

The visit also allowed the Oulder Hill pupils to raise their own questions directly with the borough’s mayor and mayoress.

This report is based on a public social-media statement and photographs shared by Mayor of Rochdale Councillor Faisal Rana. No pupils have been identified by name.

articles.title: Middleton Big Dig uncovers evidence of medieval Tonge Hall
articles.excerpt: More than 350 people, including pupils from five local schools, took part in the Middleton Big Dig and uncovered evidence of activity dating from the medieval period.
articles.summary: The community archaeological dig uncovered medieval ridge-and-furrow features and structures believed to relate to an earlier hall that stood on the Tonge Hall site before the existing Tudor building.
articles.content_html:
Evidence of medieval farming and structures connected with an earlier version of Tonge Hall has been uncovered during a community archaeological dig in Middleton.

More than 350 people took part in the Middleton Big Dig, including pupils from five local schools.

Rochdale Borough Council said participants found evidence of activity spanning from the medieval period to the present day.

Medieval landscape discovered

Among the discoveries was evidence of ridge-and-furrow farming, a landscape feature created by historic ploughing methods widely used during the Middle Ages.

Archaeologists and volunteers also uncovered structures believed to relate to the original medieval hall that existed before the Tudor building now standing on the site.

The surviving building is known as Tonge Hall.

More than 350 people involved

The project involved residents, volunteers and children from five schools, giving young people the opportunity to take part in practical archaeology and learn more about Middleton’s history.

Pupils from St Peter’s RC Primary School were among those who visited the site and helped examine finds uncovered during the dig.

Photographs shared by the council show pupils working alongside members of the archaeological team and examining material recovered from the excavation area.

Finds to be cleaned and catalogued

The objects and other material discovered during the project will now be cleaned, recorded and catalogued.

Once that work has been completed, the finds are expected to be offered to Touchstones Rochdale for inclusion in its collection.

The dig formed part of the activity programme supporting the restoration of the Broadfield Park Slopes.

The wider project has received funding from the National Lottery Heritage Fund.

This report is based on a public update and photographs published by Rochdale Borough Council following the completion of the Middleton Big Dig.

articles.title: Pupils quiz Paul Waugh about Parliament during Littleborough school visit
articles.excerpt: Pupils at St Mary’s RC Primary School in Littleborough questioned Paul Waugh about Parliament during a special assembly ahead of England’s match.
articles.summary: Paul Waugh visited St Mary’s RC Primary School in Littleborough, where pupils wearing England shirts took part in an assembly about Parliament and the history of democracy.
articles.content_html:
Pupils at St Mary’s RC Primary School in Littleborough welcomed Paul Waugh for a special assembly about Parliament and democracy.

Children asked questions about how Parliament works and discussed England’s and Rochdale’s history of democratic participation.

The visit took place ahead of England’s match on Saturday evening, with many pupils attending the assembly in England football shirts and other red-and-white clothing.

Questions about Parliament

Waugh thanked headteacher Mrs Ruane, school staff and pupils for the welcome and for the questions raised during the session.

Photographs shared following the visit showed pupils taking part in a group discussion before listening to a presentation titled All About Parliament.

The session gave children an opportunity to learn more about political representation, democratic institutions and the role Parliament plays in national life.

England colours at school

The school community also marked the occasion by wearing England tops ahead of the evening’s football fixture.

Waugh said it was good to see the pupils showing their support and expressed hope that they had learned more about the country’s democratic history during the visit.

This report is based on a public Facebook update and photographs published by Paul Waugh following his visit to St Mary’s RC Primary School in Littleborough.

Your Trust has unveiled a new Body Balance programme aimed at enhancing the physical and mental well-being of Rochdale residents. This initiative seeks to provide a comprehensive approach to health by integrating elements of yoga, tai chi, and Pilates, thereby promoting flexibility, strength, and relaxation.

Classes will be conducted at various locations throughout Rochdale, ensuring accessibility for a diverse range of participants. The sessions are designed to accommodate all fitness levels, allowing both beginners and those with more experience to benefit from the programme. Classes are set to commence on 15 July 2026, with sessions scheduled throughout the week.

Participants can register for the Body Balance classes online via the Your Trust website or by visiting local community centres. This programme is part of a wider initiative aimed at encouraging healthier lifestyles and fostering community engagement within Rochdale. It is particularly relevant in light of national health priorities that emphasise the importance of mental health and physical activity.

The introduction of structured classes is a response to the increasing concerns surrounding sedentary lifestyles and mental health challenges that have been exacerbated by the pandemic. By offering these classes, Your Trust aims to not only improve physical fitness but also to create opportunities for social interaction among residents. The social dimension of the programme is expected to bolster community spirit and strengthen support networks among participants, which could be particularly beneficial for those feeling isolated or disconnected.

For those interested in joining the Body Balance programme, further details, including class schedules and registration information, are available on the Your Trust website. Residents may also contact Your Trust directly through their contact page for any inquiries or additional support regarding the programme. This initiative represents a significant step towards enhancing community health and well-being in Rochdale, potentially leading to improved quality of life for many residents.

articles.title: MPs Seek Clarification on Release of Shabir Ahmed
articles.excerpt: There are renewed calls for legal changes to facilitate the deportation of a Rochdale rapist leader, highlighting ongoing concerns about justice and immigration policy.
articles.content_html:
ONGOING STORY — Updated 11 July 2026 at 09:33. 33 source updates have been combined into this article.

There are renewed calls for legal changes to facilitate the deportation of a Rochdale rapist leader, highlighting ongoing concerns about justice and immigration policy.

Latest update

The UK government is facing difficulties in deporting Shabir Ahmed, a convicted leader of a Rochdale grooming gang, as Pakistan has announced it will not take him back. Ahmed, who was released from prison after serving part of his sentence, has been at the centre of a political debate regarding the deportation of serious offenders.

During a recent debate in the House of Commons, local MP's called for more stringent measures to ensure that individuals like Ahmed can be deported. The discussions highlighted the complexities surrounding deportation laws and the responsibilities of foreign nations in accepting their nationals.

articles.title: Reform mayoral candidate Sian Astley stands by controversial migrant comments
articles.excerpt: Reform UK’s Greater Manchester mayoral candidate Sian Astley has defended comments suggesting Green voters and politicians should house people she described as illegal criminals, rapists and economic chancers.
articles.summary: Sian Astley said her comments were partly tongue-in-cheek and directed at supporters of open-border policies, while Labour accused Reform UK of seeking to punish communities according to how they vote.
articles.content_html:
Reform UK’s candidate for Mayor of Greater Manchester has said she stands by controversial comments suggesting supporters of other political parties should take responsibility for housing people she described as illegal criminals, rapists and economic chancers.

Sian Astley made the comments while sharing a Reform UK proposal concerning the location of migrant detention centres.

Under the proposal, facilities would be prioritised in areas represented or controlled by Green Party politicians, while areas with Reform UK-controlled councils or MPs would not receive them.

Astley suggested that Green voters, politicians from what she called the “uniparty” and councillors should instead house the people concerned themselves.

Speaking to the Manchester Evening News after the comments attracted criticism, Astley said she stood by what she had written.

She described the suggestion as partly “tongue in cheek” and argued that people who advocate open-border policies should accept the consequences of those policies.

Labour criticism

Labour Party chair Anna Turley criticised both the comments and the wider proposal.

She accused Reform UK of supporting a policy that would reward or punish communities according to how residents voted and said this undermined basic democratic principles.

Astley has not withdrawn the comments and said criticism should instead be directed at the immigration system and those entering the country unlawfully.

Greater Manchester mayoral election

Astley is Reform UK’s official candidate in the Greater Manchester mayoral election taking place on Thursday 30 July 2026.

Residents across Rochdale borough and the rest of Greater Manchester will be eligible to vote for the next mayor, with polling stations open from 7am until 10pm.

Astley is also a Manchester city councillor and leads the Reform UK group on Manchester City Council.

The original Facebook post was not supplied directly to Rochdale Daily. The comments and Astley’s subsequent defence have been reported by the Manchester Evening News and The Guardian.

Manchester Evening News source

articles.title: Sir Geoff Hurst Reflects on His World Cup Hat-Trick 60 Years On
articles.excerpt: As England prepares to face Norway, Sir Geoff Hurst recalls his iconic hat-trick in the 1966 World Cup final, marking 60 years since the memorable victory.
articles.content_html:
Sir Geoff Hurst, the first player to score a hat-trick in a World Cup final, reflected on his historic achievement as England prepares to face Norway later today. In the final minute of extra time during the 1966 World Cup final against West Germany, Hurst received a long ball from Bobby Moore and, despite feeling exhausted, struck the ball with all his might. "And of course, I mis-hit it, and it flew in," he recounted in a 2018 interview with BBC Radio 4, recalling the final goal of his hat-trick in England's 4-2 victory at Wembley.

Born in 1941 in Ashton-under-Lyne, Hurst grew up in a footballing family. His father, Charlie Hurst, was also a professional footballer, having played for clubs including Oldham Athletic, Rochdale, and Mossley before moving south to join Chelmsford City. Sir Geoff noted in a conversation with Jimmy Armfield in 1997 that it was "quite surprising" his father played as a centre-half, given he was not particularly tall.

As the nation remembers Hurst's contributions to football, BBC Radio Manchester has revisited his journey to the World Cup final and the significance of his hat-trick, which remains a defining moment in English football history.

The 1966 World Cup final is celebrated not only for Hurst's remarkable performance but also for its lasting impact on the sport in England, inspiring generations of players and fans alike.

articles.title: New Safe Haven offers support during nights out in Rochdale town centre
articles.excerpt: A new Safe Haven has been introduced in Rochdale town centre, offering practical help and a place to pause for residents and visitors during nights out.
articles.summary: The Rochdale Safe Haven provides phone charging, directions, water, first aid, emotional support and help getting home safely, with extended opening planned around major events and World Cup matches.
articles.content_html:
A new Safe Haven has been introduced in Rochdale town centre to provide practical help and reassurance to residents and visitors enjoying the town’s nightlife.

The mobile facility is positioned in Town Hall Square, beside the Gracie Fields statue, and provides a recognised place for anyone who experiences difficulty or feels vulnerable during a night out.

Visitors can use the Safe Haven to charge their phones, ask for directions, get some water or take a moment away from the busy night-time environment.

Support available can also include basic first aid, emotional support, help reporting an incident, assistance arranging a taxi and signposting to other services.

Saturday-night support

Council plans state that the Safe Haven will ordinarily operate every Saturday evening between 10pm and 3am.

The facility can also act as a trusted central point for licensed venues, street pastors, Greater Manchester Police and other organisations working to keep people safe in the town centre.

Rochdale Borough Council said residents may see more of the Safe Haven during the summer as the town hosts major events.

Extended opening hours are also planned around World Cup matches, when more people may be visiting pubs, bars and venues across the town centre.

Initial six-month trial

Funding from the Greater Manchester Combined Authority was secured to establish the service as part of wider work to improve safety in Rochdale’s night-time economy.

The initial scheme is expected to run for six months while its cost, demand and impact are reviewed.

The council has said the service could later be used more widely at events and potentially extended to other town centres across the borough.

Landowners of the abandoned Turner Brothers factory site in Rochdale, known as 'Rochdale's Chernobyl', report a rise in trespassing incidents, particularly during school holidays, raising safety concerns.

Latest update

A derelict factory site in Rochdale, referred to as 'Rochdale's Chernobyl', is experiencing rising concerns over trespassing incidents. The abandoned Turner Brothers factory, once the world's largest asbestos manufacturer, is located in a residential area just north of Rochdale town centre.

Landowners Spodden Park Ltd have reported a significant increase in trespassers, particularly during school holidays. In October 2025 alone, security was alerted to trespassing incidents more than 100 times, with estimates of 30 to 60 breaches per month, representing a 25 per cent rise during school holidays. Locals have also observed children playing in the River Spodden, which flows through the factory site.

The site remains off-limits to the public due to hazardous conditions, including the presence of asbestos fibres. Spodden Park Ltd has urged parents to educate their children about the dangers of entering the site, highlighting the risks of asbestos exposure and potential injuries from collapsing structures. They have produced a factsheet detailing the health risks associated with asbestos, including asbestosis and lung cancer.

As summer approaches, Spodden Park Ltd anticipates further increases in trespassing, particularly among local teenagers. They continue to monitor the site and work with security services to ensure safety, advising residents to report any illegal entry to the police.

Earlier developments

Concerns have been raised regarding the exclusion zone established around Rochdale's Chernobyl site. Local residents fear that the measures in place may not be sufficient to ensure safety in the area.

The exclusion zone was implemented to limit access to the site, which has been a source of environmental concern. However, some residents believe that the current restrictions are not being enforced effectively.

Residents have reported instances of people entering the zone, raising questions about the adequacy of safety protocols.

articles.title: Whitworth councillor challenges council over play park funding gap
articles.excerpt: Whitworth councillor Scott Smith has challenged Rossendale Borough Council over play park funding, claiming the town is receiving no comparable investment while substantial sums are being spent elsewhere.
articles.summary: Rossendale opposition leader Scott Smith says council officers will meet Whitworth Town Council within two months after he questioned what he described as unequal play park investment.
articles.content_html:
A Whitworth councillor has questioned why the town is not receiving what he considers the same level of play park investment as other parts of Rossendale.

Councillor Scott Smith, leader of the opposition at Rossendale Borough Council, raised the issue during question time connected to the full council meeting held on Wednesday 8 July.

“What on Earth will it take for Whitworth to receive equal treatment to every other town in this borough?”

In a social-media statement, Councillor Smith claimed that £140,000 is being spent on Edgeside Park and a further £143,000 on Whitaker Park in Rawtenstall during 2026, while Whitworth is currently set to receive no comparable play park investment.

He said a cabinet member committed council officers to meeting representatives of Whitworth Town Council within two months to put together a plan aimed at changing the position.

Councillor Smith said he intended to hold the council administration to that commitment.

Edgeside investment confirmed

Rossendale Borough Council announced on 7 July that it was moving ahead with the next phase of improvements to the top play area at Edgeside Park.

The council said the project was being supported through Pride in Place funding, with additional investment secured through the Derbyshire Environmental Fund via the Landfill Communities Fund.

However, the council's announcement did not state the £140,000 figure cited by Councillor Smith. Rochdale Daily has also not independently confirmed the £143,000 Whitaker Park figure or the claim that no comparable play park funding has been allocated to Whitworth.

Right of reply

Rossendale Borough Council is welcome to provide clarification about the funding figures and the proposed meeting with Whitworth Town Council.

articles.title: Heywood HMO protest moves to Civic Centre ahead of 11 July demonstration
articles.excerpt: Organisers have moved a protest over an HMO licence application beside Woodland Community Primary School from Heywood Memorial Gardens to Heywood Civic Centre.
articles.summary: Organisers have moved a protest over an HMO licence application beside Woodland Community Primary School from Heywood Memorial Gardens to Heywood Civic Centre.
articles.content_html:
ONGOING STORY — Updated 10 July 2026 at 23:51. 4 source updates have been combined into this article.

Organisers have moved a protest over an HMO licence application beside Woodland Community Primary School from Heywood Memorial Gardens to Heywood Civic Centre.

Latest update

A protest concerning an HMO licence application for a property next to Woodland Community Primary School is due to take place at Heywood Civic Centre on Saturday 11 July 2026 .

Organisers originally planned to demonstrate at Heywood Memorial Gardens but agreed to move the event after objections to holding it at a place of remembrance.

Heywood HMO Protesters say the campaign concerns a specific application for a property next to the school. They are asking Rochdale Borough Council, as the local licensing authority, not to grant the HMO licence.

Marlon West is expected to speak at the demonstration.

Earlier developments

The dispute became unusually public after Rochdale Borough Council used its official Facebook account to reply directly to the protest group beneath a post about the planned demonstration.

“Heywood HMO protesters councils are not responsible for asylum accommodation.”

The campaigners replied that their objection was not simply about which authority arranges asylum accommodation. Their argument is that Rochdale Borough Council still decides whether the property can legally operate as a licensed House in Multiple Occupation.

Although the demonstration has moved to the Civic Centre, the group has not withdrawn its objection to the application.

The Home Office is responsible for commissioning asylum-support accommodation and arranging placements through contracted providers. Rochdale Borough Council does not select individual occupants.

However, the council has a separate role as the HMO licensing authority. It must consider whether an application satisfies the relevant requirements, including the suitability of the property, its proposed occupancy, its management arrangements and whether the applicant and manager are fit and proper people.

If the legal licensing requirements are not met, the council can refuse the application or impose conditions.

The council's Facebook response addressed responsibility for asylum accommodation but did not directly answer the campaigners' point about the specific HMO licence application.

Update timeline

  • 10 July 2026, 23:51: Heywood HMO protest moves to Civic Centre ahead of 11 July demonstration

articles.title: Rochdale residents warned over open-water dangers and moorland fires
articles.excerpt: Rochdale Borough Council has urged residents to avoid entering open water and to keep barbecues, fires and fireworks away from the countryside during hot, dry weather.
articles.summary: The council has issued summer safety warnings highlighting the dangers of open-water swimming and the risk that even a small flame could start a serious moorland fire.
articles.content_html:
Residents across Rochdale borough are being urged to take extra care around open water and on the moors during periods of hot and dry weather.

Rochdale Borough Council has shared separate warnings about the risk of drowning and the danger of fires spreading rapidly through dry countryside.

Warning against entering open water

A water-safety campaign shared by the council warns people not to encourage friends to enter reservoirs, lakes, rivers or other open water.

“If you encourage your mates to go in the water it could be the last time you see them.”

Open water can contain hazards that are not visible from the surface, including sudden changes in depth, cold temperatures, currents and submerged objects.

The warning was produced with the Water Safety Partnership as part of efforts to prevent avoidable deaths and serious incidents.

No fires, barbecues or fireworks on moorland

The council has also reminded visitors that even a small flame can trigger a moorland fire during a prolonged dry spell.

People visiting the countryside are being told not to take disposable barbecues or camping stoves onto the moors and not to start open fires.

Visitors should also make sure cigarettes are fully extinguished and placed in a bin, while fireworks and sky lanterns should not be used in the countryside.

Council campaign material states that offences involving fires, barbecues or fireworks may result in a fine of up to £2,500 and, in some circumstances, a prison sentence.

What to do if a fire starts

Anyone who sees a fire should move away from the affected area and call 999 immediately.

People should not attempt to tackle a spreading moorland fire themselves, as conditions can change quickly and smoke can make it difficult to escape safely.

The council has asked residents and visitors to respect the countryside and help protect local wildlife, habitats and nearby communities.

This report is based on public safety material published by Rochdale Borough Council and the Water Safety Partnership.

As the week progresses, the heat will intensify, with Friday marking the hottest day, where it will feel like 32°C due to humidity. The forecast indicates minimal chances of rain throughout the week, with less than 10% expected on most days.

As the week unfolds, temperatures will remain steady, with Monday and Tuesday both expected to reach 26°C. The evenings will cool down to around 14-17°C, making for comfortable nights. This warm weather is significant for local businesses, particularly those in the hospitality and outdoor leisure sectors, as it may boost foot traffic and sales. Parks and outdoor venues are likely to see increased visitors, which can positively impact the local economy.

For those planning outdoor activities, it is advisable to check the weather regularly and prepare for high pollen counts. articles.title: Health experts issue guidance on safe fan use amid Rochdale heatwave
articles.excerpt: As temperatures rise, health experts in Rochdale provide essential advice on the safe operation of electric fans to mitigate overheating and electrical hazards.
articles.content_html:
ONGOING STORY — This report brings together confirmed developments from multiple sources.

As the UK endures a significant heatwave, health and safety experts are advising Rochdale residents on the safe operation of electric fans. This guidance, issued by High Speed Training, underscores the importance of taking precautions to prevent overheating and potential electrical hazards as households increasingly rely on fans for relief from the soaring temperatures.

Dr Richard Anderson, Director of Learning at High Speed Training, emphasised the effectiveness of fans in providing comfort during hot weather while also stressing the necessity of their proper use to ensure safety. He noted that as temperatures rise, many individuals will be reaching for their fans, but adherence to safety protocols is crucial to avoid accidents.

Residents should register new fans with their manufacturers to receive important updates regarding safety recalls. Before using a fan, it is vital to inspect it for any signs of damage, particularly focusing on the plug and cable. Fans that produce buzzing or crackling noises, or exhibit warm plugs, should be turned off immediately and examined by a qualified electrician. Regular cleaning is also important; dust accumulation on fan blades can strain the motor and increase the risk of overheating. Users must always unplug the fan before cleaning and follow the manufacturer's reassembly instructions.

Furthermore, it is advisable to avoid leaving fans running unattended or overnight while sleeping. Instead, residents might consider cooling their bedrooms prior to bedtime by operating the fan with windows open when appropriate. Proper positioning of fans is equally important; they should be placed on a flat, stable surface and kept clear of curtains or other materials that could obstruct airflow or create hazards.

Dr Anderson reiterated the significance of these safety measures, asserting that dedicating a few moments to simple safety checks can prevent fires or accidents and ensure fans operate smoothly. With the heatwave projected to persist for at least another week, adhering to these safety tips will be crucial for maintaining comfort and safety during this period. For further information or to report safety concerns, residents can contact local health and safety authorities.

articles.title: Local residents divided over trial traffic scheme at Hopwood Triangle
articles.excerpt: Rochdale Council's new traffic scheme at Hopwood Triangle aims to enhance road safety, but local residents have expressed mixed feelings about its potential impact on congestion and travel times.
articles.content_html:
Rochdale Council has launched a trial traffic scheme at the Hopwood Triangle in Heywood, aimed at improving road safety in the area. This initiative has elicited a range of responses from local residents, with some welcoming the changes while others voice concerns about possible disruptions to traffic flow.

The scheme was introduced following extensive consultations with the community, during which residents highlighted issues related to traffic management and safety. The council's objective is to address persistent concerns regarding speeding vehicles and pedestrian safety, particularly for vulnerable groups such as children and the elderly.

Since the implementation of the scheme, feedback from residents has been varied. Proponents argue that the changes are essential for reducing accidents and enhancing safety for all road users. They contend that the new measures will promote more responsible driving behaviours and create a safer environment for pedestrians.

Conversely, some residents have expressed apprehension regarding the scheme's effectiveness. Critics suggest that the alterations may lead to increased congestion and longer travel times, particularly during peak hours. There are fears that the new traffic patterns could confuse drivers who are unfamiliar with the area, potentially negating the intended safety benefits.

The council has committed to closely monitoring the trial, with plans to gather feedback from residents to evaluate the scheme's impact. A review is scheduled to take place at the end of the trial period, which will determine whether the changes will be made permanent or adjusted based on community input. Local residents can submit their views through the council's official channels, ensuring that all perspectives are considered in this important safety initiative.

The implications of this trial scheme extend beyond mere traffic management. Families, particularly those with children, may find the changes reassuring if they lead to improved safety. However, businesses in the area could experience fluctuations in customer traffic if congestion increases, potentially affecting their operations. Schools nearby may also need to adapt to any changes in travel patterns, as parents and guardians navigate the new traffic flow. Overall, the outcome of this trial could significantly influence daily life in the Hopwood Triangle, making community feedback a crucial element in shaping the future of this initiative.
